title: Loops
summary: Repeat tasks efficiently using for and while loops

sections.title: Loops are your personal assistant
sections.content: Imagine you have 100 envelopes to stamp and address. Doing each one manually would take forever. A loop is like hiring an assistant who says 'I'll do this same task for each envelope until all 100 are done.' Python has two kinds of loops. A for loop is perfect when you know how many times to repeat (like counting 1 to 10). A while loop keeps going until a condition changes (like 'keep stirring soup until it boils'). Both save you from writing repetitive code.

sections.title: Counting with a for loop
sections.content: This loop prints a countdown from 5 to 1 using range()
sections.code:
# range(5, 0, -1) generates numbers starting at 5, ending before 0, stepping down by 1
# The loop variable 'number' takes a new value each iteration
for number in range(5, 0, -1):  # Start at 5, stop before 0, count backwards
    print(number)  # Print the current countdown number
print("Blast off!")  # This runs once after the loop finishes
# Expected output:
# 5
# 4
# 3
# 2
# 1
# Blast off!

sections.title: For vs while — when to use each
sections.content: Use a for loop when you know exactly how many iterations you need — like processing every customer in a list of 50 orders. The for loop handles the counting automatically, so you can't accidentally create an infinite loop. Use a while loop when you're waiting for a condition to change — like 'keep asking for a password until the user enters the correct one.' While loops need extra caution: if the condition never becomes False, the loop runs forever (infinite loop), freezing your program. Always double-check that something inside the loop changes the condition.

sections.title: Infinite loops will crash your program
sections.content: The most common loop mistake is creating an infinite loop by forgetting to update a condition variable. Wrong: x = 0; while x < 10: print(x) — x never changes, so it runs forever. Correct: x = 0; while x < 10: print(x); x = x + 1 — x increases each time, so the loop eventually stops.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A coffee shop gives a loyalty stamp for every purchase. After 10 stamps, the customer gets a free drink. Write a program that prints each stamp number from 1 to 10 and prints 'Free drink!' when the 10th stamp is reached.
hints: Should you use a for loop or a while loop here? Why?
How can you check if the current stamp number is the 10th one?
